summary: Drew DeVault presents a comprehensive guide for video game developers who want to open source their games, arguing that releasing source code is essential for historical preservation and lasting legacy. He outlines a spectrum of approaches from source-available (bare minimum) to fully open assets under Creative Commons, with copyleft-licensed code and proprietary assets as his recommended middle ground. The post addresses common objections like cheating concerns and embarrassing code quality, dismissing both as insufficient reasons to keep games closed. He concludes with practical step-by-step instructions for publishing source code and assets.
key_points: Video games are uniquely creative software where collaborative open source benefits apply less during development, but open sourcing after release ensures historical preservation
There is a spectrum of openness: source-available, copyleft with proprietary assets (recommended), permissive license, and fully open assets — each with distinct tradeoffs
Keeping source code closed is ineffective at preventing cheating; social solutions like reporting systems work better than obscurity
The desire to 'clean up' code before releasing it is the death of many would-be open source projects — no one judges game code quality
Source-available is explicitly not the same as open source, and developers should use the correct terminology in public statements
money_quotes: If you keep the source code closed, the only conclusion is the first: enjoyed once, now forgotten.

Proprietary games tend to atrophy. Long after their heyday, with suitable platforms scarce and physical copies difficult to obtain, many games die a slow and quiet death, forgotten to the annals of time.
DOOM runs on phones, digital cameras, ATMs, even toasters! Its legacy is secure without any ongoing commitment from the original developers.
Keeping it proprietary is not going to stave off cheaters. Social solutions are better — like a system to report cheaters, or to let friends play on private servers.
The idea that it just needs to be 'cleaned up' first is the death of many projects which would otherwise have been made open source.
I assure you: your game is ready to make open source, no matter what state it's in!
Source available is not the same thing as 'open source', and the distinction is important.
key_insight: Open sourcing video games is primarily an act of historical preservation — the choice is between a game that lives forever through community ports and derivatives, or one that quietly dies when its original platform becomes obsolete.
